It feeds my anxiety, especially when I see images or stories that seem too perfect—like people living in luxurious homes or achieving impossible health results—when in truth, they are often staged or manipulated. I used to believe that what I saw online was a reflection of truth, but now I know better. My education stopped at upper secondary, and I didn’t have access to the digital tools that many younger people take for granted, so I’ve had to learn slowly and carefully. I manage the content I see by turning off notifications, limiting my time, and choosing to only follow accounts that share verified facts—like local news or health tips from trusted doctors. I also rely on my own memory and experience: if something feels too good to be true, I pause and ask myself, 'Would this have happened in real life?' I’ve found that my cautious nature, shaped by years of living through real events and hardships, helps me spot inconsistencies. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a woman in my eighties who has spent a lifetime grounded in reality and personal experience, I find the spread of fake content on social media deeply unsettling. It feeds my anxiety, especially when I see images or stories that seem too perfect—like people living in luxurious homes or achieving impossible health results—when in truth, they are often staged or manipulated. I used to believe that what I saw online was a reflection of truth, but now I know better. My education stopped at upper secondary, and I didn’t have access to the digital tools that many younger people take for granted, so I’ve had to learn slowly and carefully. I manage the content I see by turning off notifications, limiting my time, and choosing to only follow accounts that share verified facts—like local news or health tips from trusted doctors. I also rely on my own memory and experience: if something feels too good to be true, I pause and ask myself, 'Would this have happened in real life?' I’ve found that my cautious nature, shaped by years of living through real events and hardships, helps me spot inconsistencies. I don’t feel the need to engage with misinformation, and I avoid sharing anything that makes me uneasy. The world moves fast online, but I believe in the value of slow, thoughtful observation. For me, staying grounded in reality is not just a habit—it’s a necessity.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona’s high neuroticism and anxiety make them particularly vulnerable to emotional distress from false or exaggerated content. Their high openness and conscientiousness support a careful, reflective approach to media consumption. Their limited formal education means they rely on personal judgment and lived experience rather than technical or digital literacy. The opinion integrates these traits by emphasizing caution, personal reflection, and emotional resilience in navigating social media.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, cautious, grounded, slightly anxious but wise
citations: World Health Organization, 2023 - Mental Health and Digital Media
